body: sample 1: sample 2: sample 3: sample4: sample 5: sample 6: sample 7: sample 8: sample 9: sample 10: ## Summary - mirror the `dataset-srj18` repository structure with original KiCad boards, generated Circuit JSON, Simple Route JSON, CommonJS/TypeScript exports, validation, and a local viewer - expose the publishable public package as `@tscircuit/dataset-srj24@0.0.1` with no `private` flag - add ten complete, production-oriented Apache-2.0 hardware designs pinned to immutable source commits - document each board's purpose, interfaces, measured routing properties, source, and license in the README - generate the dataset with the published `kicad-to-circuit-json@0.0.115` and `kicadts@0.0.52` fixes - license original repository code and documentation under MIT while preserving Apache-2.0 for the boards and derived board datasets ## Board corpus 1. Jetson Nano Baseboard 2. HDMI to MIPI CSI-2 Bridge 3. SDI to MIPI CSI-2 Bridge 4. Thunderbolt to PCIe Adapter 5. M.2 to PCIe x4 Adapter 6. CM4 Baseboard LVDS Adapter 7. PMOD I3C Sensor Board 8. D1600E PSU Breakout Board 9. M.2 to OCuLink Adapter 10. Dual I-PEX CSI Interposer The set excludes testbeds, simulation boards, ICT fixtures, archived repositories, and projects described upstream as experimental. ## High-complexity sample The Jetson Nano Baseboard is the deliberately difficult case: 391 components, 1,516 pads, 1,759 traces, 794 vias, eight copper layers, and 827 SRJ connections. ## Conversion and provenance - checked-in `.kicad_pcb` files are exact upstream downloads - every source and license URL is pinned to a 40-character commit - parser-only compatibility normalizations are recorded per board in `source-files.json` - all ten boards regenerate with zero converter warnings and require no missing-port repairs with converter `0.0.115` and parser `0.0.52` - regenerated outputs for samples 001, 003, and 007 include the merged conversion fixes ## Licensing Original repository code and documentation are MIT unless otherwise stated. The source boards in `kicad_pcb/` and generated board data in `circuit-json/` and `samples/` remain Apache-2.0 and are not relicensed by the repository-level MIT license. The repository includes the Apache-2.0 text in `LICENSES/Apache-2.0.txt` and immutable attribution in `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md` and `source-files.json`. No pinned source has a root `NOTICE` file. ## Validation - `bun install --frozen-lockfile` - `bun run generate` - `bun run test` - `bun run build` - `npm pack --dry-run --json` - `node --check scripts/download-and-generate.mjs` - `node --check scripts/validate.mjs` - `git diff --check` - all ten regenerated boards pass KiCad GenCAD logical connectivity, pcbnew physical connectivity, DRC connectivity assertions, and IPC-2581 trace-endpoint checks (55 assertions, zero failures)

title: Add option to hide schematic in analog simulation viewer
description: Adds a hideSchematic prop to AnalogSimulationViewer, allowing users to choose between displaying the circuit schematic or focusing solely on the simulation traces.
body:
## Summary

- add a `hideSchematic` prop to `AnalogSimulationViewer`
- keep the existing combined schematic and simulation graph as the default
- render only the standalone simulation graph when `hideSchematic` is enabled

## Why

Consumers need to choose whether the analog simulation view includes the circuit schematic or focuses only on the simulation traces.

## Impact

Existing callers keep the current combined rendering without any changes. Callers can opt into graph-only rendering with:

```tsx
<AnalogSimulationViewer circuitJson={circuitJson} hideSchematic />
```

Schematic color overrides continue to apply when the schematic is shown.

## Validation

- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`
- verified the generated declaration exposes `hideSchematic?: boolean`
- verified the generated bundle selects the standalone graph renderer when the flag is true

title: Improve high-effort residual repair yield
description: What changed attach exact PCB pad and via centers to Pipeline 7 DRC errors so local repairs target the colliding copper prioritize directed local repairs, terminal snapping, via movement, pair movement, and sparse obstacle detours preserve the established repair strategy order until the residual set is truly sparse score exploratory candidates with geometry-only DRC, then run the full benchmark-equivalent DRC before every accepted move clone and reconvert only the one or two routes changed by a candidate stop after an effort-scaled plateau with no DRC-count reduction, while resetting the plateau whenever a productive repair is found report candidate, validation, and plateau statistics for benchmark diagnosis  Why High effort previously evaluated every candidate with the full relaxed DRC suite. Trace continuity dominated that cost even though ordinary doglegs and segment shifts cannot change terminal continuity. The solver also cloned and reconverted the entire board for moves that touched only one or two routes. The expanded sparse-repair portfolio could additionally displace older productive strategies on boards that still had many residual errors. That reduced quality on dataset 18 sample 16, while the larger raw candidate cap allowed score-only sweeps to consume the remaining benchmark timeout. The new scorer filters candidates with exact copper-geometry DRC, but full relaxed DRC remains the acceptance authority. The solver therefore cannot accept a geometry improvement that regresses continuity or another benchmark check. Experimental obstacle and pair-dogleg moves are limited to one or two residual issues, after the established strategies have reduced the board to a genuinely sparse state.  Dataset 18 validation All samples and checkpoints were run sequentially at 50x effort. full sample 16 on parent commit 3090686: 2 final DRC errors; residual rerouting used 249 iterations and 59.4s full sample 16 on this branch: the same 2 final DRC errors; residual rerouting used 249 iterations and 10.0s (about 83 faster) fixed sample 10 checkpoint: 4 - 3 DRC errors, stopped on a count plateau after 132542 candidates in 11.7s fixed sample 16 checkpoint: 7 - 2 DRC errors, naturally exhausted useful candidates after 248542 attempts in 10.8s 20-candidate sample 4 probe: preserved the same 19 - 12 DRC reduction while reducing phase time from 5.99s to 4.68s The full sample-16 run completed normally. Productive count reductions continue to reset the plateau, while unproductive score-only search cannot consume the full high-effort cap indefinitely.  Checks bun run build 17 focused residual-repair and effort-scaling tests full sequential Pipeline 7 dataset-18 sample 16 at 50x effort This PR remains intentionally stacked on 1670 so the original effort-scaling PR stays separate.
body:
## What changed

- attach exact PCB pad and via centers to Pipeline 7 DRC errors so local repairs target the colliding copper
- prioritize directed local repairs, terminal snapping, via movement, pair movement, and sparse obstacle detours
- preserve the established repair strategy order until the residual set is truly sparse
- score exploratory candidates with geometry-only DRC, then run the full benchmark-equivalent DRC before every accepted move
- clone and reconvert only the one or two routes changed by a candidate
- stop after an effort-scaled plateau with no DRC-count reduction, while resetting the plateau whenever a productive repair is found
- report candidate, validation, and plateau statistics for benchmark diagnosis

## Why

High effort previously evaluated every candidate with the full relaxed DRC suite. Trace continuity dominated that cost even though ordinary doglegs and segment shifts cannot change terminal continuity. The solver also cloned and reconverted the entire board for moves that touched only one or two routes.

The expanded sparse-repair portfolio could additionally displace older productive strategies on boards that still had many residual errors. That reduced quality on dataset 18 sample 16, while the larger raw candidate cap allowed score-only sweeps to consume the remaining benchmark timeout.

The new scorer filters candidates with exact copper-geometry DRC, but full relaxed DRC remains the acceptance authority. The solver therefore cannot accept a geometry improvement that regresses continuity or another benchmark check. Experimental obstacle and pair-dogleg moves are limited to one or two residual issues, after the established strategies have reduced the board to a genuinely sparse state.

## Dataset 18 validation

All samples and checkpoints were run sequentially at 50x effort.

- full sample 16 on parent commit `3090686`: 2 final DRC errors; residual rerouting used 249 iterations and 59.4s
- full sample 16 on this branch: the same 2 final DRC errors; residual rerouting used 249 iterations and 10.0s (about 83% faster)
- fixed sample 10 checkpoint: 4 -> 3 DRC errors, stopped on a count plateau after 132/542 candidates in 11.7s
- fixed sample 16 checkpoint: 7 -> 2 DRC errors, naturally exhausted useful candidates after 248/542 attempts in 10.8s
- 20-candidate sample 4 probe: preserved the same 19 -> 12 DRC reduction while reducing phase time from 5.99s to 4.68s

The full sample-16 run completed normally. Productive count reductions continue to reset the plateau, while unproductive score-only search cannot consume the full high-effort cap indefinitely.

## Checks

- `bun run build`
- 17 focused residual-repair and effort-scaling tests
- full sequential Pipeline 7 dataset-18 sample 16 at 50x effort

This PR remains intentionally stacked on #1670 so the original effort-scaling PR stays separate.

body: the double pill shape wasn't parsed correctly, the other 3 issues used to look like this: ## What - use KiCad's absolute board-level pad angle when transforming custom SMD primitives - convert custom through-hole pads into polygonal plated holes, preserving custom copper and oval drills - emit overlapping same-number custom plated holes before their ordinary partners so neither drill is painted over - keep pad conversion orchestration small by moving rotation, ID allocation, contour parsing, custom copper geometry, custom plated-hole creation, and overlap ordering into focused typed modules - use the public `kicadts` API instead of private parser fields in the changed implementation - add minimized SRJ24 fixtures and strict SVG snapshots for samples 001, 004, 006, and 008 - add focused unit coverage for stable overlapping-pad ordering - make strict comparison opt-in for the shared SVG fixture helper so legacy snapshot behavior is unchanged ## Root cause Pad positions remain footprint-local in `.kicad_pcb`, but pad angles are already absolute. The converter reapplied the footprint rotation to custom primitives, producing the wrong orientation. Custom plated through-hole primitives also had no conversion branch and were dropped. Sample004 constructs each L-shaped shield slot from two overlapping `SH` pads. Circuit JSON keeps those holes separate, and the SVG renderer draws each pad's copper and drill together. title: Fix Monaco resolution for root-relative workspace imports
description: Fixes incorrect reporting of root-relative workspace imports in Monaco, restoring accurate diagnostics, autocomplete, and code navigation for tscircuit projects.
body:
## Summary

Fix Monaco incorrectly reporting root-relative workspace imports such as `"imports/SWITCH"` as missing, even when the target file exists in the editor workspace.

This restores accurate diagnostics, autocomplete, and code navigation for tscircuit projects using root-relative imports.

## Bug reproduction

Given these Monaco workspace models:

- `file:///index.circuit.tsx`
- `file:///imports/SWITCH.tsx`

The following import previously produced a false `Cannot find module` diagnostic:

```tsx
import { SWITCH } from "imports/SWITCH"
```

Monaco stored workspace files under `file:///` URIs but resolved bare module specifiers without a matching workspace base URL.

## Fix

- Configure `file:///` as the workspace `baseUrl`.
- Apply consistent compiler options to both TypeScript and JavaScript language services.
- Keep compiler-option construction browser-independent and reusable.
- Preserve relative imports and external package resolution.

## Regression coverage

Added a regression test that recreates the Monaco workspace URI layout and verifies:

```text
imports/SWITCH → file:///imports/SWITCH.tsx
```

## Validation

- 56 tests passing
- Typecheck passing
- Package build passing
- Formatting and diff checks passing

## Summary

Improves workspace navigation by replacing the flat file selector with interactive, searchable breadcrumbs.

- Show file-path breadcrumbs for every supported file type
- Show TypeScript and JavaScript symbol breadcrumbs based on the active cursor position
- Navigate sibling files, directories, and document symbols from breadcrumb dropdowns
- Add fuzzy file search and keyboard navigation directly inside breadcrumb menus
- Preserve access to the active file when hidden workspace paths are filtered
- Use consistent, type-specific icons across breadcrumbs, Quick Open, and the file tree
- Simplify the editor header and remove the redundant sidebar search interface

## UX impact

This significantly reduces the effort required to move through large or deeply nested workspaces. Users can understand their current file and symbol context, jump between nearby files, search a subtree, and navigate code symbols without leaving the editor header.

The breadcrumbs also remain useful for Markdown, JSON, and other non-code files instead of disappearing outside TypeScript and JavaScript files.

## Implementation

- Adds reusable utilities for constructing and flattening sorted file trees
- Reuses the existing Quick Open fuzzy-matching behavior
- Integrates document symbols from Monaco’s TypeScript language service
- Consolidates file and symbol icon selection into a shared utility
- Follows the repository’s naming, parameter, casing, and module-scope helper conventions

## Testing

- Added tests for nested file-tree construction, sorting, merging, and flattening
- Added tests for file icon selection and fallback behavior

title: Preserve fragmented pad reachability
description: Preserves route-net identity for connected copper represented by adjacent SRJ obstacle rectangles and ensures that generated test plans are executed correctly without missing files.
body:
## Summary

- preserve route-net identity for connected copper represented by adjacent SRJ obstacle rectangles
- apply the metadata after node-dimension subdivision, and only when an endpoint has no direct routing exit
- add focused unit coverage and an SRJ24 sample 6 static-reachability regression
- run every generated Bun test-plan shard so test files cannot be silently skipped when shard assignments change

## Root cause

The J3 shield polygon is rasterized into adjacent obstacle strips. Node subdivision splits those strips into obstacle-only regions, but the derived regions lose the pad's net identity. The tiny-hypergraph loader then removes the anonymous full-obstacle regions, isolating two GND terminals.

The CI workflow also replaced node 1's generated test plan with a single long-running test. Files assigned to the rest of node 1 were never executed, and adding two tests shifted stale snapshots into node 2.

## Validation

- `bun run build`
- 32 scoped Pipeline 7 tests across 24 files
- SRJ24 sample 6 polygon-pad reachability regression
- existing e2e3 routing snapshots remain unchanged
- generated test-plan audit: 315 files, 315 unique planned entries, none missing or duplicated

description: Preserves PCB terminal IDs from TinyHypergraph port-point pathing through high-density routing and stitching for Pipeline 7, ensuring earlier repair and optimization behavior remains unchanged and locking final route endpoints by their preserved IDs.

## Summary

- preserve PCB terminal IDs from TinyHypergraph port-point pathing through high-density routing and stitching for Pipeline 7
- carry the identities as inert route-order metadata so earlier repair and optimization behavior remains unchanged
- lock final route endpoints by their preserved IDs instead of choosing an orientation from endpoint distance
- throw on malformed connections, duplicate terminal IDs, and unknown route terminal IDs

## Why

This follows up on @0hmX's review in #1659. The terminal-lock step previously inferred endpoint identity after simplification by comparing direct and reversed distances. That can associate a route endpoint with the wrong PCB port. Setting `pcb_port_id` on route points too early also changes optimization behavior, so this keeps a Pipeline-7-only sidecar until the existing terminal-lock boundary.

## Validation

title: Extract workspace editor logic into composable hooks and modular breadcrumb components
description: This PR decomposes the WorkspaceCodeEditor and Breadcrumbs components into focused, independently testable units by extracting logic into hooks and modularizing components. It introduces new hooks for model management, navigation, and symbol tracking, and reorganizes the test suite for better maintainability. No breaking changes are introduced, and the public API remains consistent.
body:
## What changed

`WorkspaceCodeEditor` (previously ~600 lines) and `Breadcrumbs` (~450 lines) mixed
model management, navigation, keyboard handling, and rendering in single files.
This PR decomposes them into focused, independently testable units:

### New hooks (`src/hooks/`)
- **`useWorkspaceModelManager`** — owns the Monaco model manager: keeps a live
  model per text file so the TypeScript language service resolves cross-file
  imports project-wide, and defers TS worker-graph initialization until the
  workspace settles.
- **`useWorkspaceNavigation`** — cross-file "go to definition" routed through
  `onFileSelect`, model swapping with per-file cursor/scroll state preservation,
  and pending-reveal application when the target file becomes active.
- **`useDocumentSymbols`** — tracks the active model's symbol outline and the
  symbol path containing the cursor, refetching on content changes.
- **`useWorkspacePalettes`** — mutually exclusive Quick Open / workspace search
  state plus the global Ctrl/Cmd+P and Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+F shortcuts.

### Breadcrumbs modularization (`src/components/breadcrumbs/`)
`BreadcrumbSegment`, `FileTreeMenu` (with fuzzy file search), and
`SymbolMenuItems` are now standalone components; sibling lists per path depth
are computed in a single tree walk so cursor-move re-renders stay cheap.

### API consistency
- Multi-parameter callbacks converted to named-parameter objects
  (e.g. `onReplace({ matches, replacement, useRegex })`), keeping function
  signatures at two parameters or fewer per the handbook style guide.
- Named closures hoisted to module scope for testability.

### Test suite: one test per file
The suite (57 tests) is reorganized so each file contains exactly one `test()`,
grouped in per-module directories (`tests/workspaceSearch/`, `tests/quickOpen/`,
`tests/tsciPackageSupport/`, …) with shared setup hoisted into `tests/fixtures/`.
Assertions are unchanged.

## Breaking changes

None. The public exports (`WorkspaceCodeEditorProps`, `WorkspaceCodeEditorHandle`,
`EditorFile`, monaco utilities) are untouched; the only changed signature
(`WorkspaceSearchProps.onReplace`) is internal and not exported from the package
entry point.

## Verification

- `bun run typecheck` — clean
- `bun test` — 57 pass / 0 fail across 57 files
- `biome format` — applied

title: Fix analog simulation canvas background
description: Fixes the analog simulation canvas background to derive from the root SVG background color or the graph-only background fill, ensuring the canvas does not reveal the host applications background during panning or zooming.
body:
## Summary

- derive the analog pan/zoom canvas background from either the root SVG `background-color` or the graph-only `.background` fill
- fall back to the effective schematic background override and then the default `circuit-to-svg` schematic palette instead of transparency
- add regression coverage for legacy graph-only SVGs, custom backgrounds, palette fallback, and combined-mode precedence

## Root cause

`AnalogSimulationViewer` only searched for an inline `background-color` on the root SVG. Graph-only output from `circuit-to-svg@0.0.390` paints its background with a full-size `.background` rectangle instead, so the graph itself was correct while the surrounding pan/zoom canvas remained transparent.

`circuit-to-svg@0.0.391` now also exposes the root style via tscircuit/circuit-to-svg#629, but the viewer should not depend on one transitive SVG representation. This change recognizes both contracts and provides a palette-aware fallback.

## Impact

Panning or zooming graph-only analog simulation output no longer reveals the host application's background. The canvas stays synchronized with default and custom graph backgrounds. Combined schematic-plus-simulation output remains unchanged because its root SVG background continues to take precedence.

## Validation

- `bun test` (4 passing)
- `bun run format:check`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`

title: test(repro): reproduce false-positive overlap error for non-overlapping circular plated holes
description: Adds a minimal repro for a bounds-overlap bug affecting circular plated holes.
body:
## Summary
Adds a minimal repro for a bounds-overlap bug affecting circular plated holes.

Two chips are placed with circular plated holes (`shape="circle"`) whose outer
diameters do not actually overlap on the board — this is confirmed visually
and via the `"CIRCLES DO NOT OVERLAP"` note text baked into the fixture.
Despite this, `renderUntilSettled()` produces a
`pcb_footprint_overlap_error` between `U1.pin1` and `U2.pin1`, indicating the
bounds/overlap-detection logic for circular plated holes is computing an
incorrect bounding region (likely using outer diameter/radius math that
doesn't match actual circle-to-circle distance checks).

## Repro
```tsx
<board width="12mm" height="12mm">
  <chip name="U1" pcbX={-1.6775} pcbY={1.27} footprint={<footprint><platedhole shape="circle" holeDiameter={3.81} outerDiameter={6.198} /></footprint>} />
  <chip name="U2" pcbX={1.6775} pcbY={-1.27} footprint={<footprint><platedhole shape="circle" holeDiameter={1.016} outerDiameter={1.88} /></footprint>} />
</board>
```

## What this PR does NOT do
This only adds the failing-case test and its snapshot to lock in the current
(buggy) behavior for follow-up work — it does not change the overlap-detection
algorithm itself. Flagging this now so the actual fix (in the circle-overlap
bounds check) can be tracked and picked up separately.

## Test plan
- [x] `bun test tests/repros/repro153-circular-plated-hole-bounds-overlap.test.tsx`
- [x] Snapshot committed showing the false-positive `pcb_footprint_overlap_error`

title: Add minimal repro for explicit netlabels rendering as long schematic traces
description: Adds a minimal regression case demonstrating that explicit V3_3 and GND netlabels can render as long schematic traces to a distant component despite schMaxTraceDistance.
body:
## Summary

Adds a minimal regression case demonstrating that explicit `V3_3` and `GND` netlabels can render as long schematic traces to a distant component despite `schMaxTraceDistance`.

## Reproduction

The circuit contains only:

- One two-pin chip with explicit power netlabels
- One distant decoupling capacitor connected to those nets
- A schematic snapshot capturing the unexpected full-width traces

## Validation

- `bun test tests/repros/repro152-explicit-netlabels-render-long-traces.test.tsx`
- `bunx biome check tests/repros/repro152-explicit-netlabels-render-long-traces.test.tsx`

## Solver fix

The algorithm fix and solver-level regression coverage are in [tscircuit/schematic-trace-solver#685](https://github.com/tscircuit/schematic-trace-solver/pull/685).

title: Use rectdiff obstacle connectivity in Pipeline 7
description: Removes the Pipeline 7 metadata repair pass while preserving physical obstacle ownership at the SRJ-to-mesh boundary and preventing synthetic replacement metadata from polluting component-local regions.
body:
## Summary

- update `@tscircuit/rectdiff` to commit `ba0f5e0` from [rectdiff#138](https://github.com/tscircuit/rectdiff/pull/138)
- remove the Pipeline 7 `FragmentedObstacleConnectivitySolver` stage and reconstruction helpers
- clear connectivity metadata from synthetic component replacement regions before topology merging
- add focused SRJ23 sample 3 and boundary-level connectivity regressions
- retain the SRJ24 sample 6 polygon-pad reachability regression
- update affected visual snapshots

## Why

RectDiff now emits one obstacle-backed mesh node per SRJ obstacle and copies its `connectedTo` metadata directly. Pipeline 7 can consume physical obstacle ownership without rediscovering adjacent fragments.

Synthetic component replacement obstacles are different: their connectivity aliases describe a temporary global-solve placeholder spanning multiple component nets. That metadata must be cleared with the replacement obstacle/target flags before component-local topology is overlaid. Otherwise topology merging unions every component alias into neighboring pad regions, leaving them ambiguously owned and statically unreachable.

## Impact

This removes the Pipeline 7 metadata repair pass while preserving physical obstacle ownership at the SRJ-to-mesh boundary and preventing synthetic replacement metadata from polluting component-local regions.

## Validation

- SRJ23 sample 3 static reachability regression
- SRJ24 sample 6 polygon-pad reachability regression
- focused synthetic replacement metadata unit regression
- affected snapshot tests
- build and type generation

title: Preserve obstacle connectivity in mesh nodes
description: Preserves obstacle connectivity in mesh nodes by emitting one obstacle-backed mesh node for each SRJ obstacle and copying the obstacles connectedTo names directly to _connectedTo, while removing connectedToByZ and _connectedToByZ.
body:
## Summary

- emit one obstacle-backed mesh node for each SRJ obstacle
- copy the obstacle's `connectedTo` names directly to `_connectedTo`
- remove `connectedToByZ` / `_connectedToByZ`
- add a public-pipeline regression for colocated obstacles on different layers

## Root cause

`finalizeRects` grouped obstacle output using only XY geometry. Distinct SRJ obstacles could therefore be merged when they shared bounds despite belonging to different layers or nets.

## Impact

Free-space generation still uses the per-layer obstacle indexes. Final obstacle nodes now come directly from SRJ, preserving each obstacle's layer and connectivity identity without extra per-layer metadata.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/obstacle-connectivity.test.ts`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`

title: Fix cursor navigation silently dropped or misapplied during file model attachment
description: Fixes cursor navigation issues where navigation requests were either silently dropped or misapplied during file model attachment, ensuring that navigation requests are properly handled and applied to the correct file model.
body:
## What
Workspace file navigation (sidebar click, breadcrumb "reveal", go-to-definition,
search "reveal match") had two independent code paths for applying the
resulting cursor reveal/focus, and they had drifted:

- `revealLocation` applied the reveal without checking that the target file's
  model was actually attached to the editor yet — if called during a model
  swap, it could apply position/focus against the wrong (stale) file's model.
- `selectFile` had that attachment check, but on failure it silently dropped
  the navigation request with no fallback — the reveal just never happened.

## Fix
Both paths now route through a single mechanism: navigation requests record
intent and a lone effect applies the reveal once the target model is
confirmed attached, retrying via the model-swap effect if it isn't ready yet.
No entry point can lose a request or apply it against the wrong model anymore.

## Testing
Manually verified in the Cosmos `TscircuitWorkspace` fixture:
- Sidebar file switch (cross-file) — model swap + reveal correct
- Reselecting the already-active file — no crash, no dropped reveal
- Ctrl+click go-to-definition across files — correct model swap, reveal,
  and focus, confirmed via console (no errors)

No behavior change to the public API (`revealLocation` return contract,
`onFileSelect` prop) — internal only.

title: Fix crash when creating a file reuses a disposed model
description: Fixes crash when creating a new file in the workspace editor due to reusing a disposed model.
body:
## Summary
- Creating a new file in the workspace editor could crash the host app with `_BugIndicatingError: Model is disposed!`
- `getOrCreateModel` looked up an existing model via `getModel(path)` and called `syncModel` on it without checking whether the model had already been disposed, so a stale reference in the internal cache would throw on `model.getValue()`/`model.setValue()`
- Now `getOrCreateModel` drops disposed models from the cache and creates a fresh model instead of trying to reuse them

## Test plan
- [x] Reproduced the crash locally by creating a new file in the workspace editor and confirmed it threw `Model is disposed!` from `syncModel`
- [x] Applied the fix and confirmed creating a new file no longer crashes and the file opens normally
